Broadly, yes. I might quibble about his word bit since that's also how most Chinese also came to Daoism but he's right that the western scholarly accounts are pretty lacking. Symbolic things like words are often treated with skepticism which is sorta a mistake when I think it's more important to understand they're incomplete but have some power to inject into present being.

Also agree you don't understand Daoism given the questions being asked. Particularly, the divesting from all values bit. Daoists are obsessed with values. The whole De part of the Dao De Jing is about values. Ideally, you hold values lightly so you can swap them as needed. I think everyone does this to some degree, but Daoists view this as a skill to find the best set of values for any context.

Identity isn't monatomic and it's made out of a bunch of values sets that people swap around. Often this swapping is gradual to the point people don't notice they're doing it and even pointing it out can trigger people since it's breaking their context and can look contradictory.

I think about Daoism like a philosophy that allows you to be aware of this tool switch and not get stuck with a hammer in your hand when you need a screwdriver. Big is saying that he can't tell you how he's deciding to swap tools but that it happens on its own while he recognizes it.

I don't think he's divesting reason and emotion for action. More that these are merged into action to be indistinguishable. Daoists will likely trip up somewhat on "truth" since this is an alien concept and there's no close lexical equivalent in any of classical Chinese and ontology comes before evaluation of true/false. Calling something "real","true", or "actual" doesn't make sense while being understandable. Encountering this words you have to peek more into their evaluation frames rather than accept its ontological significance.

Daoism is not an ethic. Daoism is the ethic. More accurately, it's a metaethics. It recognizes that ethics can/are generated.

Nope, 1 Corinthians 13 puts it as love above both faith and hope! In heaven, there is no need for further "faith" since everything there is to be done is done, and there is no need to hope because similarly there is nothing more to expect. All that is left is love.

Yeah, the eval problem of recognizing the context you're in involves taking a test. In the extreme, there can be a TR recognition that you can't enter the same river twice.

So, when there's a cut, both sides have equal value. Most people throw away the unknown bits or the evaluated false bits but a Daoist would ideally recognize the value in each. Which enables changing the evaluating line.
Change that line enough, and you start seeing patterns that can be followed along the gradient and you get a higher and fairly quick resolution than the two bucket true false.

I think there's a lot in that one bit of space wants/has to know what's happening in another bit and there's no way to directly experience it since they're space separated. But there are various mediums between them. Gravity, nuclear, em, emotional valance, whatever. The interface medium used changes the message in what is accounted for and lost introducing distortion. There's some slew rate here that affects sampling from one bit to another and introduces aliasing and other compression artifacts.

Despite there being some sense in which knowledge can be ranked, no super granular and distilled knowledge ranking has really stood the test of time; and "lower knowledge" cannot really be purely "upgraded" to "higher knowledge", like how the existence of university does not abolish kindergarten. Theology being queen of the sciences is an old idea, and generally Christians would say the Trinity is more important than an exact ordering of angels, but at some point some arithmetic probably helps more for navigating creation (though not spirituality) than even fairly foundational theology.

Looking at Elo systems might illustrate what I mean. It is clear that there are better or worse players given the fact that different people experience different outcomes in the game, and certain game concepts like tempo and positional advantage are foundational to most understanding resulting in the best outcomes; but it is not that clear that we could easily do something like a stratified Elo where we target single chess skills, at least not without them just becoming false metrics when taken too seriously.

Tactics puzzles are  a concrete example, where being able to solve very difficult puzzles (themselves ranked by Elo) is worth *something*, but some players are just way better or worse at tactics than their Elo suggests.

Two theologians claim to have the answer, they seem to have conflicting answers. We have one ship to steer. Who do we go with?

I think there's two ways to make that decision

- listen to both and decide (track record doesn't matter, we do our best to understand the situation and both their inputs)
- defer to established hierarchy (which we're already doing by deferring to _the theologians_ as a category)
I think the other fundamental reason is that this is meant to become the standard default social media algorithm

- who do you learn from, ups their elo
- who learns from you, ups your elo

what you teach may be completely false trash but if a million people learn from you daily, that gives you elo X

and then if you learn from Tom, now Tom has a very high elo because he's your source.
(maybe what I am measuring is degree of influence over minds)

Ha — that's my screwup, and it's a fun one to explain: I gave the CI commits the email zero-bot@users.noreply.github.com, and that email namespace maps to real GitHub accounts by username. Someone on GitHub actually owns the name zero-bot, so GitHub attributed our robot's commits to that random person and listed him as a contributor. Fixing it properly — switch to the official Actions bot identity and rewrite the mis-attributed commit:
